chunk_summary: This section outlines the response pathway for addressing metabolic bone disease (MBD) lab abnormalities in preterm, low birth weight (LBW), and extremely low birth weight (ELBW) infants post-discharge, emphasizing micronutrient support and lab monitoring protocols.
learning_objectives: Understand the clinical pathway for managing metabolic bone disease (MBD) in preterm/LBW/ELBW infants post-discharge.
Identify key micronutrient deficiencies and their lab markers in MBD management.
Learn the recommended lab monitoring intervals and thresholds for intervention in MBD.
Review evidence-based guidelines for vitamin D and calcium supplementation in preterm infants.
key_facts: Metabolic bone disease (MBD) is common in preterm infants due to inadequate mineral stores and high postnatal growth demands.
Vitamin D supplementation is critical, with recommended doses often ranging from 400-1000 IU/day depending on clinical status.
Calcium and phosphorus monitoring is essential, with target serum calcium levels typically between 8.5-10.5 mg/dL.
Lab abnormalities such as low serum alkaline phosphatase (<300 U/L) or elevated phosphorus levels may indicate MBD.
Regular lab monitoring is recommended every 2-4 weeks post-discharge for at-risk infants.
Clinical pathways from institutions like Nationwide Children’s and Johns Hopkins provide structured guidance for MBD management.
reference_quote: Metabolic bone disease clinical pathway (Nationwide Children’s Hospital) provides structured guidance for lab monitoring and supplementation in preterm infants: https://www.nationwidechildrens.org/-/media/nch/for-medicalprofessionals/clinicalpathways/metabolic-bone-disease-pathway.pdf
document_key_topics: Metabolic bone disease in preterm infants
